description_long: This object is a large, upright Celtic cross made of stone, featuring intricate interwoven knotwork patterns carved into its front surface. The cross is set within a circular ring, a hallmark of traditional Celtic design, and the front face displays detailed relief work with symmetrical motifs. The rear side is plain and unadorned, showing only the basic outline of the cross and circle. The sides are relatively narrow and flat, with some subtle surface texture and faint markings. The stone appears weathered, with a matte, slightly rough finish, suggesting exposure to the elements. This type of object is commonly used as a guard stone or boundary marker in urban or historical settings.
description: A large, stone Celtic cross with intricate knotwork carvings on the front and a plain, weathered back, typically used as a guard stone or marker.
description_view_0: The front view shows detailed Celtic knotwork carvings and a circular ring intersecting the cross.
description_view_1: The side view reveals the cross's thickness and flat, unadorned edges with subtle surface texture.
description_view_2: The rear view displays a plain stone surface with the cross and circle outline, lacking any decorative carvings.
description_view_3: The opposite side view is similar to view 1, showing the object's thickness and unembellished stone surface.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to parks, historical sites, or along urban sidewalks as a boundary marker or decorative guard stone, often near pedestrian pathways or at intersections.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright and securely anchored to prevent tipping or movement.
attribute_wear_patterns: Likely to show surface smoothing and minor chipping along edges due to weathering and occasional contact with pedestrians or maintenance equipment. Discoloration and moss or lichen growth may occur on the lower sections from prolonged exposure to moisture.
attribute_historical_markings: Intricate Celtic knotwork carvings and circular motifs are present on the front face, with no visible inscriptions or text.
